text: The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), dividing it just below the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure. A medial to lateral plane is developed in front of the aortic fascia.
The surgeon performs dissection from the medial side under the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), identifying and separating the pancreas from the colonic splenic flexure.
The surgeon releases the omentum from the transverse colon, carefully separating the omental tissue from the colonic surface.
The surgeon encounters adhesions from a previous laparoscopic gastric sleeve surgery while continuing dissection on the left paracolic side, aiming for full mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon makes an initial incision at the level of the sacral promontory to identify and dissect the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon opens the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane and carefully dissects cranially alongside the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The IMA is traced to its origin and divided using hemolog clips.

The surgeon identifies the superior rectal artery to enter the TME plane posteriorly, following the vessel to open the posterior plane. Dissection is performed deeply in both anterior and posterior directions.

The surgeon begins dissection in the anterior plane, starting one centimeter above the peritoneal reflection. The lateral dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) is visibly challenging, requiring careful maneuvering.
The surgeon performs early anterior and posterior dissection, facilitating the identification and approach to the lateral dissection plane.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection down to the level of the pelvic floor, following the established TME plane.

The surgeon performs dissection in a sequential manner, starting with posterior dissection, followed by right lateral, anterior, and then left lateral dissection, maintaining a consistent approach throughout the procedure.
The surgeon divides the posterior midline roughing at the back to facilitate the stretching of the rectum.
The surgeon performs a digital rectal examination to identify the level of the tumor within the rectum, using tactile feedback to assess its position.
The surgeon identifies the vagina and continues the dissection of the rectovaginal septum towards the pelvis, carefully separating the tissue layers between the rectum and vagina.
The surgeon carefully pulls the rectum out of the pelvis using a plastic tube placed around the distal rectum, applying gentle traction to avoid damaging the specimen.
The surgeon uses a digital rectal examination to aid in visualizing the pelvic floor and identifying the tumor in the distal rectum.
The surgeon prepares to apply a rectal stapler to transect the rectum, ensuring precise alignment and closure at the distal end.
The surgeon performs robotic transection of the mesocolon, facilitating the subsequent extraction of the specimen through the anus.
The surgeon places a lone star retractor to enhance exposure for dissection, improving visualization of the tumor in the distal rectum.

The surgeon closes the anus using a post-transducer to ensure a free distal margin. A small elliptical incision is made above the sphincters, and the dissection proceeds until the connection with the abdominal team is established.
The video shows a decrease in intra-abdominal pressure, likely observable through changes in the abdominal cavity or surgical field.
The surgeon extracts the specimen transanally through an Alexis wound protector. Following this, an enhancing coloanal anastomosis is performed using interrupted stitches of Tuo Vicryl.
